questions.question: What is the primary function of an encoder in a digital circuit?
modelAnswer: An encoder converts information from one format to another.
Specifically, it converts 2^n lines of input into an n-bit binary code.
It performs the inverse operation of a decoder.

questions.question: Explain the problem of priority ambiguity in a standard 4-to-2 encoder and describe how a priority encoder solves it.
modelAnswer: A standard encoder malfunctions if more than one input line is active (high) at the same time, as it cannot determine which input to encode.
A priority encoder includes internal logic that assigns priority to each input, typically with the highest-order input having the highest priority.

When multiple inputs are active, it encodes the input with the highest priority and often includes a valid output (V) to indicate if any input is active, distinguishing a valid code from the all-zeros output.

questions.question: Design and describe the logic for an 8-to-3 priority encoder, including its truth table, Boolean expressions for its outputs (including a valid bit), and a brief discussion on its practical applications.
modelAnswer: The truth table has 8 inputs (I7 highest priority to I0 lowest) and outputs a 3-bit binary code (A2, A1, A0) and a valid bit (V). The outputs encode the highest priority active input. V=1 if any input is active, V=0 only if all inputs are 0.
Boolean expressions: A2 = I4 + I5 + I6 + I7; A1 = I2 + I3 + I6 + I7; A0 = I1 + I3 + I5 + I7; V = I0 + I1 + I2 + I3 + I4 + I5 + I6 + I7.
Practical applications include interrupt request handling in microprocessors (where multiple devices request service simultaneously and the highest priority must be identified) and keyboard encoding.
